nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reef-associated waters with coral and rocky substrates; tends to hide beneath ledges and inside small caves during the day, becoming more active at dusk and at night. Reported depth range is roughly 1-130 m, most commonly 1-20 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding almost exclusively on small fishes in the wild, with some intake of crustaceans. In captivity, generally accepts meaty foods such as krill, mysis, frozen silversides, chopped shrimp and squid; newly imported specimens have been known to refuse prepared foods initially and may require live feeder shrimp or small live fish to initiate feeding.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth
delicate or slow-moving tankmates that cannot tolerate water-quality stress

summary: The Golden Stripe Soapfish (Grammistes sexlineatus) is an Indo-Pacific reef predator whose skin glands release grammistin, a soap-like toxin that can prove fatal to other tank inhabitants when the fish is stressed. Generally considered an advanced-care species best kept singly in a large FOWLR system.
careNotes:
The Golden Stripe Soapfish is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa east to the Marquesas and Mangareva islands, north to southern Japan and south to New Zealand. It tends to inhabit coastal reefs with caves, ledges and overhangs at depths of roughly 1-130 m (typically 1-20 m), where it hides during the day and ambushes small fishes and crustaceans at night.

Toxin warning (critical husbandry concern): The species has specialised dermal glands that release grammistin, a soap-like skin toxin that gives the family its common name. When the fish is stressed, injured or dies, grammistin can leach into the surrounding water and has been reported to kill other tank inhabitants. The shipping water this species arrives in should NEVER be added to the display tank. Drip-acclimate the fish itself only, and consider a quarantine system that can be flushed if the animal becomes stressed. Because of this toxin risk, it is generally advised to keep this species singly with robust tankmates rather than in mixed reef systems with delicate invertebrates.

Colour change with age: Juveniles display clean white-on-dark longitudinal stripes that progressively subdivide and break up into dashes and spots as the fish matures, meaning adults can look considerably different from the young specimens typically offered in the trade.

Behaviour: Tends to be nocturnal and reclusive, spending much of the day inside rockwork. Ample live-rock structure with caves and overhangs is generally recommended to provide secure daytime retreats.

Feeding transition: Newly imported individuals may initially refuse prepared foods. Target feeding with a feeding stick and offering live saltwater feeder shrimp tends to work well to initiate feeding; most specimens have been known to transition to frozen meaty foods over time.

Reef compatibility: Will generally consume ornamental shrimp and any fish small enough to be swallowed. Corals and sessile invertebrates are typically left undisturbed, but the toxin risk alone makes this species a poor candidate for prized mixed-reef displays.
breedingNotes: Has been reported as a broadcast spawner in the wild; captive breeding is rarely documented and likely impractical in a home aquarium setting.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source pulled from collection page https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/groupers; the species' product page was navigated to directly. Listed at 12 inches max, 180 US gal minimum, Expert Only care, aggressive temperament, reef safe with caution, with explicit grammistin toxin warning about shipping water.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Grammistidae, Perciformes), maximum length 30 cm TL, Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to Marquesas, southern Japan to New Zealand), temperature range 25.2-29 C, depth 1-130 m, and toxin-producing dermal glands with reports of ciguatera. Notes ontogenetic color change.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30 cm), minimum tank 500 L, reef safe with caution, nocturnal and shy behaviour, and the toxin-release warning that shipping water must never be added to a display aquarium.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30 cm), temperature 25-29 C, predatory carnivore diet, lists many alternate common names. Lists 1500 L minimum tank which is markedly larger than other sources; treated as an outlier on the conservative end.

fish.breedingNotes: Broadcast spawner in the wild; captive breeding is rarely documented.
